coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based paper sensor with smartphone for quantitative detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Yuwei
Cao, Lulu
Zhang, Xiyan
Jiao, Rui
Ou, Dexin
Wang, Yang
Zhang, Danfeng
Shen, Yizhong
Ling, Na
Ye, Yingwang
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, food poisoning events caused by Vibrio parahaemolyticus have shown an upward trend. Therefore, there is a growing need for a rapid, sensitive and simple detection method for V. parahaemolyticus. The aim of this study was to develop a novel aptamer-based paper sensor for the rapid, sensitive, portable and specific detection of V. parahaemolyticus in seafood through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle. The capture probes for this strategy were made by chemically coupling carbon dots (CDs) to amino-modified single-stranded DNA. Switches were double-stranded DNA formed by hybridization of anti-V. parahaemolyticus aptamers (aptamer) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)-probes. Once V. parahaemolyticus was introduced, the aptamer in the double-stranded DNA bound specifically to the target bacteria and the sensor was ‘On’, releasing the AuNPs-probes to bind to the capture probes. The fluorescence color of the CDs changed significantly from bright blue to colorless because of the FRET between CDs and AuNPs. As expected, when filter paper was used as the assay liquid carrier, an ingenious FRET-based paper sensor was presented for accurate field reporting of V. parahaemolyticus, with a linear range of 101-106 cfu/mL. This strategy has a detection limit of 8.9 cfu/mL for V. parahaemolyticus in culture and 6.7 × 101 cfu/mL in artificially contaminated cod samples. The results indicate that this study has good potential as a new portable sensor for the detection of V. parahaemolyticus in complex food matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
On-site assay
Paper sensor
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Vibrio parahaemolyticus.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of nanoprobes to Vibrio parahaemolyticus
FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone on-site detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus
Optimization of the FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone
Fluorescence response of nanoprobes to Vibrio parahaemolyticus
On-site fluorescence imaging of Vibrio parahaemolyticus by FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone
On-site fluorescence imaging of V. parahaemolyticus in real samples
Testing the specificity of paper sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for the on-site detection of FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone.

Feasibility verification. (A) DLS analysis of AuNPs, (B) DLS analysis of AuNPs-Probes, (C) DLS analysis of CDs, (D) DLS analysis of CDs-cDNA, (E) DLS analysis of CDs-cDNA after hybridization with AuNPs-probes, (F) Fluorescence spectra of different conditions.

Optimization of the method. Optimization of (A) cDNA concentration on the sensitivity of paper sensor, (B) volume ratio of CDs and aptamer-AuNPs-probes on paper sensor sensitivity, (C) quenching time on paper sensor sensitivity, (D) pH on paper sensor sensitivity. Error bars: the standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

The relationship between the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus in PBS solution and the fluorescence intensity of nanoprobes. (A) Fluorescence image of nanoprobes in the presence of V. parahaemolyticus of different concentrations, (B) Fluorescence spectra of V. parahaemolyticus at different concentrations, (C) Quantitative ability of nanoprobes for V. parahaemolyticus in PBS. 1 to 6 represented the concentrations of V. parahaemolyticus were 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106 cfu/mL, respectively, and 0 represented 0 cfu/mL. Error bars: the standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

Relationship between the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus in PBS solution and the B/R on paper sensor. (A) Fluorescence images of paper sensor in the presence of V. parahaemolyticus with different concentrations, (B) B/R value of paper sensor in the presence of V. parahaemolyticus of different concentrations, (C) Quantitative ability of paper sensor for V. parahaemolyticus in PBS. 1 to 8 represented the concentrations of V. parahaemolyticus were 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108 cfu/mL, respectively, and 0 represented 0 cfu/mL. Error bars: the standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

Sensitivity and selectivity of paper-based sensor for V. parahaemolyticus in cod samples. (A) Fluorescence image of paper sensor in the presence of V. parahaemolyticus of different concentrations in cod samples, (B) B/R value of paper sensor in the presence of V. parahaemolyticus of different concentrations in cod samples, (C) Quantitative ability of paper sensor for V. parahaemolyticus in cod samples, (D) Specificity of Smartphone-assisted paper sensor for selected foodborne pathogenic bacteria. Error bars: the standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
Comparison of different detection methods for Vibrio parahaemolyticus.

row.entry: Propidium monoazide PCR- lateral-flow test strip
Rapid technique based on recombinase aided amplification

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based paper sensor with smartphone for quantitative detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Tunxi Road 193, Hefei, Anhui province, 230009, China.
Corresponding author. Tunxi Road 193, Hefei, Anhui province, 230009, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, food poisoning events caused by Vibrio parahaemolyticus have shown an upward trend. Therefore, there is a growing need for a rapid, sensitive and simple detection method for V. parahaemolyticus. The aim of this study was to develop a novel aptamer-based paper sensor for the rapid, sensitive, portable and specific detection of V. parahaemolyticus in seafood through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle. The capture probes for this strategy were made by chemically coupling carbon dots (CDs) to amino-modified single-stranded DNA. Switches were double-stranded DNA formed by hybridization of anti-V. parahaemolyticus aptamers (aptamer) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)-probes. Once V. parahaemolyticus was introduced, the aptamer in the double-stranded DNA bound specifically to the target bacteria and the sensor was ‘On’, releasing the AuNPs-probes to bind to the capture probes. The fluorescence color of the CDs changed significantly from bright blue to colorless because of the FRET between CDs and AuNPs. As expected, when filter paper was used as the assay liquid carrier, an ingenious FRET-based paper sensor was presented for accurate field reporting of V. parahaemolyticus, with a linear range of 101-106 cfu/mL. This strategy has a detection limit of 8.9 cfu/mL for V. parahaemolyticus in culture and 6.7 × 101 cfu/mL in artificially contaminated cod samples. The results indicate that this study has good potential as a new portable sensor for the detection of V. parahaemolyticus in complex food matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus) is a gram-negative, salt-loving bacteria found mainly in coastal areas (Peng et al., 2015; Preetham et al., 2020). Under optimal growth conditions, the bacteria have a much shorter growth time than other bacteria such as Pseudomonas, Listeria monocytogenes and Salmonella. Consumption of food containing V. parahaemolyticus can lead to abdominal pain, vomiting and watery stools, and even death in severe cases (Jiang et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2020). Numerous studies have shown that V. parahaemolyticus has an extremely wide transmission pathway and is the main cause of food poisoning associated with marine products in countries such as Korea and Japan and the United States (Lee et al., 2022). In addition to seafood, livestock and poultry meat, salted vegetables, salted eggs, freshwater fish, etc. are found to have the presence of V. parahaemolyticus. Therefore, the establishment of a good specificity and sensitivity, easy operation the detection of V. parahaemolyticus method, to prevent and control the contamination and spread of V. parahaemolyticus in food (especially seafood products) is of great significance.

Biosensor systems relying on smartphone analysis can provide a fast, portable, and easy-to-operate detection platform for sensitive and high-throughput real-time analysis of trace biological samples (Shen et al., 2022). Therefore, among many analytical tools in the field of rapid food safety detection, biosensors based on smartphone analysis systems have more performance advantages such as accuracy, portability, and ease of operation. Based on the types of analytes and the principles of different detection techniques, smartphone-based analytical systems can be divided into four main categories, namely, optical sensing types, electrochemical sensing types, molecular biology sensing types, and immunological sensing types (Cheng et al., 2018). These smartphone-based analytical systems were investigated to enable the detection of various analytes, including food allergens (Lin et al., 2017), cyanobacterial toxins (Li et al., 2019), heavy metal ions (Xiao et al., 2020) and malondialdehyde (Fashi et al., 2020), as well as food-borne pathogenic bacteria (Wang et al., 2019). Currently, the types of optical sensing based on smartphone analysis systems typically combine naked eye visible or fluorescent nanomaterials with biomolecules to form a visual or fluorescent colorimetric detection system using a smartphone, targeting mainly whole bacteria or cells. In addition, since optical biosensing devices can be easily added to mobile phone cameras using paper as a carrier, many smartphones have been developed with integrated optical biosensing technology (Yang et al., 2022).

In recent years, fluorescence-based assays have become very popular for the detection of foodborne hazards due to their ease of preparation, rapid response, high sensitivity and high specificity. (Wu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2018). Among these, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a phenomenon that occurs between two closely linked fluorescent molecules and is widely used in biological spectral sensing techniques. As the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, the fluorescence intensity of the donor is quenched and the fluorescence intensity of the acceptor is enhanced when the distance between the two molecules is within 10 nm (Yao et al., 2007). As a result, FRET has an efficient response even in trace detection. To date, the traditional donors for FRET have mainly been organic fluorescent dyes. Unfortunately, the inherent characteristics of most organic dyes, with their narrow excitation spectra, broad emission spectra and susceptibility to photobleaching and photolysis, limit their widespread use (Alidagi et al., 2019; Ibisoglu et al., 2020). As a new type of fluorescent donor with adjustable emission wavelength and high excitation efficiency, quantum dots are attracting more and more attention (Takkinen & Zvirbliene, 2019). However, quantum dots require multiple synthesis steps, unstable preservation, relatively high toxicity and fluorescence yields need to be improved (Xu et al., 2016). Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel fluorescent donor have received widespread attention due to their unique physical properties and applications in a variety of fields of biosensing. They are ideal FRET donors because of their easy synthesis, high water solubility, strong fluorescence emission, resistance to photobleaching and good biocompatibility (Da et al., 2022). In addition, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are able to simplify detection methods due to their ease of surface functionalization. Also, its strong surface plasmon absorption in the near infrared (NIR) to infrared (IR) region is considered to be an effective fluorescent quenching agent (Chai et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2010). Based on the quenching property of AuNPs against CDs, Liu, and Fu et al. (Fu et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2017) proposed a two-dimensional optical sensing platform, a fluorescent nanosensor with “on-off-on” signal to detect arginine in urine and thiram in hawthorn, respectively, all with good results. However, it is noteworthy that most of these strategies for the proximity of AuNPs and CDs require complex chemical modifications and the application of this property is rarely mentioned in the detection of pathogenic bacteria. In order to solve this problem, Yao et al. (Yao et al., 2021) identified Staphylococcus aureus by strand displacement reaction combined with the quenching characteristics of AuNPs on CDs. Unfortunately, the efficiency of strand displacement reaction is lower than the specific substitution efficiency of the bacterium for the aptamer in double-stranded DNA (Chen et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2021), and the specific long DNA sequence doesn't make the donor any closer to the acceptor in order to achieve the conditions for the strand displacement reaction. In addition, most of these biosensor detection systems use fluorescence readers to display the fluorescence response in a liquid environment, and the large size of the instrument limits its application to on-site detection.

In this study, we used double-stranded DNA (aptamer-AuNPs-probes) made from aptamers hybridized with AuNPs-probes as a switch for the sensor, avoiding the limitations of strand displacement reactions in FRET applications and the extraction of specific nucleic acids from target bacteria. The use of DNA hybridization shortens the distance between fluorescent donor and acceptor, avoiding cumbersome chemical modifications. Signal transduction imaging was performed using filter paper as a carrier built into the smartphone to enable on-site detection. These images were then quickly converted to digital red-green-blue (RGB) color values for on-site quantitative analysis of V. parahaemolyticus using the installed color selector application. Through the comprehensive performance analysis of paper sensor we developed, its effectiveness was verified. The results shown that the sensor exhibited promising applications for the efficient and rapid detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria in food. As far as we know, this is the first report of a novel, sensitive, portable and FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone for the rapid quantitative detection of V. parahaemolyticus in seafood.

Gold chloride tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), trisodium citrate were supplied by Chengdu Kelong Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Tris-(2-carboxyethyl)-phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), Citric acid and dl-cysteine were obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). Whatman No.1 filter paper was purchased from Jieyi Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1 × PBS (pH = 7.6), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) obtained from Sigma Chemical Company (St. Louis, Mo, USA). Vibrio parahaemolyticus (ATCC 17802), Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 7644), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 25922), Salmonella typhimurium (ATCC 14028), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 65389), Escherichia coli (ATCC 8739), Enterobacter sakazak. (ATCC 29544) were provided by Guangdong Institute of Microbiology (Guangdong, China). The DNA probes were purchased in Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). The DNA sequence was described below:
Anti-Vibrio parahaemolyticus aptamer (Duan et al., 2013): 5′- ATA GGA GTC ACG ACG ACC AGA ATC TAA AAA TGG GCA AAG AAA CAG TGA CTC GTT GAG ATA CTT ATG TGC GTC TAC CTC TTG ACT AAT -3′
aDNA: 5′-CGT GAC TCC TAA AA–(CH2)6–SH-3′
cDNA: 5′–NH2–(CH2)6-AGG AGT CAC G -3′

The fluorescence spectra of this experiment were obtained by PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkinlemer, Shanghai, China). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of AuNPs and CDs were obtained using JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan). The absorption spectra of AuNPs and AuNPs-probes were obtained by TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) between nanostructures was measured using Zetasizer NANO ZS90 (Malvin instrument, Worcestershire, UK).

V. parahaemolyticus ATCC 17802 was incubated overnight in tryptic soy broth (TSB) containing 3% NaCl and other foodborne pathogens were incubated overnight in TSB (shaking at 37 °C, 180 rpm). After that, the enriched bacteria were scribed onto LB agar plates at 37 °C overnight. In order to obtain purified colonies, a single colony of V. parahaemolyticus was selected from the Petri dish and cultured in TSB containing 3% NaCl for 12 h at 37 °C and the other colonies were inoculated in TSB for 12 h at 37 °C. After incubation, the traditional plate counting method was used to determine the concentration of bacteria. Prior to use, the broth cultures were centrifuged at 5000 × g for 5min to make particles and resuspended in sterile phosphate buffered saline (PBS = 0.1 M, pH = 7.6). Bacterial samples were diluted 10-fold with PBS to a concentration range of 0 to 108 cfu/mL.

The synthesis of carbon dots was slightly improved on previous research (Zhang et al., 2018). First, 30 ml of ultrapure water was added to 2 g citric acid and 2 g dl-cysteine, and after ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, obtain a uniformly dispersed solution. Then transfer the dissolved mixture to a polytetrafluoroethylene sealed autoclave at 180 °C for reaction for 1.5 h. After the reaction was completed, it was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the obtained brown liquid was dialyzed in a 500–1000 Da cellulose dialysis membrane for 5 days to synthesize CDs solution.

12 μL of the CDs solution obtained above was added to 338 μL of ultrapure water. Then, 5 mg NHS and 10 mg EDC were added to 350 μL of diluted CDs aqueous solution and incubated at 27 °C for 30 min (Song et al., 2018). Subsequently, 50 μL of cDNA (10 μM) was added to 350 μL of the activated CDs solution and suspended at 27 °C for 2 h to synthesize the CDs-cDNA capture probes.

The synthesis of AuNPs was similar to previous work (Huang et al., 2022). First, 1 mL of 1% HAuCl4 was added to 100 mL of distilled water and bring to a boil, then quickly added 2.5 mL of 1% trisodium citrate and continue boiling for 15 min. After heating was stopped, kept stirring until it was naturally cooled to room temperature. Finally, the resultant citrate-stabilized AuNPs colloids about 15 nm were prepared and stored at 4 °C for further use. We appropriately improved the AuNPs-probes based on these previous researches (Wang et al., 2017). 100 μL of sulfhydryl-modified aDNA (10 μM) was annealed at 95 °C for 5 min and then cooled at 4 °C for 5 min. Treated aDNA (10 μM, 100 μL) was incubated with 1 mL of AuNPs at 37 °C for 22 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 20 min to remove free DNA from the supernatant, and the pellet was then resuspended in 400 μL of PBS solution to recover the AuNPs-probes.

To form a stable double stranded DNA of AuNPs-probes-aptamer, 2 mM MgCl2 and Tween 20 (m/v = 0.01%) were added to a mixture containing 200 μL of AuNPs-probes and 100 μL of aptamer (10 μM), which was then incubated at 60 °C for 10 min. The mixed solution was incubated in a water bath (37 °C, 1 h) and cooled at room temperature for 20 min. The cooled solution was centrifuged (12,000 × g, 20 min) to remove the supernatant and resuspended in 200 μL PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.6). Kept the above synthesized nanoprobes of AuNPs-probes-aptamer and CDs-cDNA at 4 °C until the use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of nanoprobes to Vibrio parahaemolyticus
ce:section.ce:para: In PE tubes, 200 μL AuNPs-probes-aptamer double-stranded DNA was added to PBS buffer (50 μL, pH = 7.6) containing serial concentrations of V. parahaemolyticus (10°-108 cfu/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Afterwards, the mixture was incubated with 350 μL of CDs-cDNA for 45 min at 37 °C and then the emission spectra of each sample were observed using a fluorescence spectrometer at a single-wavelength excitation of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone on-site detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to fluorescence imaging of liquid systems, solid paper-based assays are more portable and commercially viable. In this study, we used filter paper strips as the vehicle for performing imaging, built into a smartphone. For on-site detection of V. parahaemolyticus, 60 μL of the “2.7. Fluorescence response of nanoprobes to V. parahaemolyticus” assay solution were uniformly dropped onto the strips. After approximately 90 s of natural air drying, the portable power supply was activated and a 365 nm UV lamp was lit, thereby acquiring V. parahaemolyticus-dependent fluorescence images via the smartphone's built-in camera. These images were then quickly converted to digital RGB color values for quantitative analysis using the installed color selector application.

Cod samples are purchased from local supermarkets in Hefei, China, and then confirmed by standard culture method that there was no V. parahaemolyticus. The samples were cut into 25 g segments, placed in sterile sample bags, and manually inoculated with V. parahaemolyticus of different concentrations (0-108 cfu/g). Each gradient sample was mixed with 25 mL PBS and homogenized for 2.5 min. 60 μL of homogenate follows the above procedure (Section “2.8. FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone on-site detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus").

Since there are many interfering bacteria in real food samples, the specific recognition ability of the sensor should be studied. The blank sample solution and the sample solution containing the same concentration (106 cfu/mL) of Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Salmonella typhimurium, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacter sakazakii, Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus were analyzed using the sensor according to section “2.8. FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone on-site detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus”. The B/R value of each sample solution was recorded, where the B/R value of the blank sample solution was recorded as B0/R0 and the sample solution containing foodborne pathogens was recorded as B1/R1. The specificity of the paper sensor was indicated by the ratio of B1/R1 to B0/R0 as a basis for judgement.

Fig. 1 illustrated an effective and simple FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone. In this platform, we first hybridized the aptamer with AuNPs-probes to form a double-stranded DNA, and when V. parahaemolyticus was present, the aptamer specifically combined to V. parahaemolyticus, which led to the release of AuNPs-probes (Ren et al., 2021). Then AuNPs-probes were mixed and incubated with CDs-cDNA and the incubated mixture was added dropwise to filter paper. Because AuNPs have a large extinction coefficient and a broad energy absorption band in the visible range, they would overlap with the emission spectrum of CDs for fluorescence quenching that change the paper strip from bright blue to colorless. Based on this property, nanoprobes with fluorescent response to V. parahaemolyticus were developed. Through integrating with the smartphone-based fluorescent device (Fig. S1), visual fluorescence images of V. parahaemolyticus with different concentrations were collected after the built-in 365 nm UV lamp was turned on. The color selection application of smartphone further converted fluorescent images into digital RGB signals, thus allowing quantitative analysis tests of V. parahaemolyticus by B/R values with high sensitivity, selectivity and outstanding resistance to interference.

As shown in Figs. S2A and B, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results show that the synthesized AuNPs and CDs have good dispersion in the free state with particle sizes of approximately 15 nm and 4 nm, respectively. After modification with DNA the hydrated particle size of AuNPs-probes and CDs-cDNA increased from 51.23 ± 1.39 nm and 11.63 ± 0.12 nm to 69.54 ± 1.42 nm and 15.69 ± 0.19 nm (Fig. 2 A, B, C and D), respectively. In addition, as shown in Fig. S3, the maximum absorption peak of the UV absorption spectrum of AuNPs was 524 nm, while the maximum absorption peak of AuNPs-probes was 530 nm. This shift in the maximum absorption peak was due to the change of refractive index on the surface of AuNPs-probes, a phenomenon that also indicates the successful preparation of a uniformly dispersed AuNPs-probes. Ultimately, as shown in Fig. 2E, after we incubated AuNPs-probes and CDs-cDNA, the hydrated particle size of the mixture increased to 102 ± 1.78 nm, which indicates the formation of hybrid nanostructures.

To further verify the feasibility of the scheme, we performed fluorescence assays on nanoprobes under different situations. As shown in Fig. 2F, the CDs-cDNA fluoresce intensely blue at 419 nm. When aptamer-AuNPs-probes was added to the CDs-cDNA, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly, which was acceptable. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity was greatly quenched after the addition of a mixture of V. parahaemolyticus incubated with aptamer-AuNPs-probes to the CDs-cDNA. In addition, the spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and the UV absorption spectra of AuNPs ensures that energy transfer from CDs to AuNPs is feasible if they are spatially close (Fig. S4). Thus, all results provide evidence that our nanoprobe has been successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: RGB color mode is a color standard in the industry. It obtains a wide variety of colors by varying the three color channels of red (R), green (G), blue (B) and superimposing them on each other. This standard includes almost all the colors perceived by human vision and is one of the most widely used color systems. (Fan et al., 2021; Ruttanakorn et al., 2021; Singh et al., 2020). When analyzing the paper sensor in this study, it was found that the ratio of the blue channel color value B to the red channel color value R (B/R) in the color selector application installed on the smartphone showed a good response to fluorescent images of different concentrations of V. parahaemolyticus, therefore B/R was used as the signal value for the paper sensor in this study. In order to obtain the best detection efficiency, the cDNA concentration, the volume ratio of CDs-cDNA to aptamer-AuNPs-probes, the quenching time, and the pH of the buffer were optimized. In the optimization experiment, we took 200 μL AuNPs-probes-aptamer was incubated with 50 μL of V. parahaemolyticus (106 cfu/mL) at 37 °C for 45 min as the quenching solution. Simultaneously, 60 μL of the CDs solution was dropped onto filter paper, then the UV lamp in the smartphone-integrated device was switched on to excite the paper strip, the fluorescent image was collected using the camera in the smartphone and the B/R value of the color in the image was read by the color selection application and recorded as B0/R0. The color change of the paper strip reflects the quenching efficiency, and in the optimization process we express the color change of the paper strip was expressed as Δ(B/R) (Δ (B/R) = B0/R0-B1/R1, where B0/R0 is the B/R of the original CDs and B1/R1 is the B/R of the CDs after quenching).

The concentration of cDNA plays an important role in the quenching of the paper sensor. If the cDNA concentration was too low, only some CDs would be functionalized, resulting in only a small amount of CDs-cDNA binding to the AuNP probe, making the quenching efficiency low. Conversely, too high a concentration of cDNA would result in a large amount of free cDNA in the system, which would compete with the CDs-cDNA for the AuNPs-probes, also resulting in a reduction in quenching efficiency. For cDNA optimization, 350 μL of CDs solution was developed and coupled to 50 μL of cDNA at different concentrations (4, 6, 8, 10, 12 μM). After taking 350 μL of the CDs-cDNA and incubating it with the quenching solution respectively, 60 μL of the mixed solution was taken and dropped onto the filter paper. The UV lamp of the smartphone-integrated device was switched on to excite the paper strip and the camera in the smartphone collects the fluorescent image, which was converted into a B/R value by the color selection application and recorded as B1/R1. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the color change (Δ(B/R)) of the paper sensor was enhanced with the increased in the concentration of the cDNA until it reached the highest value at 10 μM and decreases at 12 μM. These results might be due to an excess of free cDNA binding to the AuNPs-probes, thereby reducing the binding efficiency of the CDs-cDNA and AuNPs-probes.

In addition to the concentration of cDNA, the volume ratio of CDs-cDNA to AuNPs-probes plays an important role in the quenching efficiency. When performing the optimization of the volume ratio of aptamer-AuNPs-probes to CDs-cDNA, we took 10 μM of cDNA and coupled it with 350 μL of CDs, then added different volumes (aptamer-AuNPs-probes: CDs-cDNA = 1:0.75, 1:1, 1:1.25, 1:1.5, 1:1.75, 1:2, 1:2.25, quenching solution as described above, containing 200 μL aptamer-AuNPs-probes) of CDs-cDNA to the quenching solution for incubation. Finally, 60 μL of the reaction solution was dropped onto filter paper, and the fluorescent image B1/R1 values were recorded via the color selection application. As shown in Fig. 3B, where the Δ(B/R) of the paper sensor was enhanced until the volume ratio of CDs-cDNA to aptamer-AuNPs-probes was 1:1.75.

For incubation time optimization, we took 10 μM of cDNA and 350 μL of CDs for incubation to synthesize CDs-cDNA, then 350 μL of CDs-cDNA and quenching solution were incubated for different times (15, 30, 45, 60, 75 min), and took 60 μL of the reaction solution and dropped it onto the filter paper. The filter paper was then excited by a smartphone-integrated device and the B1/R1 values of the fluorescence images were recorded. As shown in Figs. 3C and 45 min was most suitable as the optimal incubation time when a steady state was reached. The reaction system developed in this study was carried out in PBS buffer. It is well known that the pH of the buffer is one of the most important factors affecting the sensitivity of the sensor. When carrying out the optimization of the pH of the buffer, a stable range (6.4, 6.8, 7.2, 7.6, 8.0, 8.4) was chosen to employ different pH values for the optimization. As shown in Fig. 3D. The color change was most pronounced when the pH was 7.6 and Δ(B/R) was 2.33.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of nanoprobes to Vibrio parahaemolyticus
ce:section.ce:para: To our knowledge, AuNPs have excellent quenching properties for CDs due to FRET. Given this unique property, we hybridized the aptamer with AuNPs-probes to form double-stranded DNA as a switch to trigger the sensor. As expected, when V. parahaemolyticus was present in solution, the AuNPs-probes were in the on state and CDs would be quenched at the emission peak of 419 nm. Under optimal conditions, we found a good fluorescence dependence of the prepared nanoprobes on the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus (Fig. 4 A). As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity was inversely proportional to the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus from 101 to 106 cfu/mL. By further plotting the relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 419 nm (F/F0, F was the fluorescence intensity at 419 nm of the sample to be tested and F0 was the fluorescence intensity at 419 nm of the blank sample) and the concentration of V. parahaemolyticus, we further concluded that F/F0 = −0.1091 lg [V. parahaemolyticus (cfu/mL)] + 0.932 (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9908, Fig. 4C). Thus, through 3σ/slope calculated the low detection limit of 5.2 cfu/mL, where σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity at 419 nm of the blank sample after eleven repeated measurements. These results clearly proved the high sensitivity of the established nanoprobes for the detection of V. parahaemolyticus in pure culture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site fluorescence imaging of Vibrio parahaemolyticus by FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the analytical tests described above we could be confident that the nanoprobes system has significant sensitivity for the detection of V. parahaemolyticus on the bulky fluorescence spectrometer. However, due to the large size of the instrument, fluorescence detection in liquid environments had not been possible for the on-site detection of V. parahaemolyticus. In order to ensure food safety and environmental health, higher requirements were put forward for the on-site monitoring of V. parahaemolyticus. With this in mind, we next developed an automated portable paper sensor based on FRET using a smartphone. Compared with sensor detection in liquid environment, solid detection using paper-based interface reaction is more commercially feasible and portable. As shown in Fig. S1, the platform of the portable smartphone integration device consists of eight main parts: the carrier, the UV lamp, the UV lamp holder, the filter tank, the filter paper, the filter equipped, the smartphone and the mobile power. When assembling the device, the step a is to mount the UV lamp onto the UV lamp holder; the step b is to insert the mounted UV lamp into the UV lamp mounting hole of the carrier; the step c is to insert the filter tank into the filter tank mounting hole of the carrier as a reaction centre to hold the filter paper in place; the step d is to insert the filter equipped into the filter equipped mounting hole below the UV lamp mounting hole; the step e is to fix the filter paper in the filter tank; the step f is to mount the smartphone with camera into the smartphone mounting slot of the carrier to visualize the fluorescence image on-site; the step g is to connect the UV lamp to the mobile power. Once the device was assembled, the target bacteria were added to the nanoprobe mixture and incubated, they were dropped on the surface of the paper strip for about 90 s. After turning on the built-in 365 nm miniature UV lamp, the paper strip was excited and visual fluorescence imaging of V. parahaemolyticus was performed on a smartphone with a camera. The concentration dependence of B/R values on V. parahaemolyticus was observed by converting the fluorescence images to RGB chromaticity values using the installed color selector application. As shown in Fig. 5 A, as the concentration of target bacteria increases, the blue fluorescence of paper base gradually became lighter, and the corresponding B/R value was inversely proportional between 0 and 106 cfu/mL (Fig. 5B). It could be seen from Fig. 5C that the sensor has a superior linear relationship between 101 to 106 cfu/mL, with the linear equation was B/R = −0.563 lg [V. parahaemolyticus (cfu/mL)] + 5.198 (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9872), and a low detection limit of 8.9 cfu/mL. These results clearly demonstrate the practical potential of FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone in the field detection of V. parahaemolyticus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site fluorescence imaging of V. parahaemolyticus in real samples
Testing the specificity of paper sensor
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to assess the cross-reactivity of sensors. As shown in Fig. 6D, in order to test our proposed automated portable FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone was selective, we tested the B1/R1 values of different food-borne pathogenic bacteria (106 cfu/mL) under the same test conditions and used the ratio of the B1/R1 values to those of the background signal (B0/R0) as a basis for judgement. Finally, our proposed paper sensor was proved to be highly specific for V. parahaemolyticus.

In summary, we had successfully developed FRET-based paper sensor with smartphone for accurate on-site detection of V. parahaemolyticus in food. In this study, the aptamers were hybridized to AuNPs-probes to form double-stranded DNA for specific identification of the target bacteria. AuNPs-probes displaced from the double-stranded DNA by V. parahaemolyticus are used to bind to the CDs-cDNA to cause a fluorescence quenching. The target bacteria effectively triggered the nanoprobes system to change from bright blue imaging to colorless imaging at 365 nm single excitation wavelength, producing a fluorescent response for V. parahaemolyticus. We use the specific binding of target bacteria and aptamers to release AuNPs, allowing the unencumbered design of capture probes and AuNPs-probes, greatly reducing the distance between donor and recipient without complex chemical modifications and cumbersome nucleic acid amplification substitution procedures. With the aid of a stylish smartphone, the internally mounted camera allows rapid collection of on-site fluorescence images related to V. parahaemolyticus residue levels and converts them to digital color RGB values. The strategy has a detection limit of 8.9 cfu/mL for V. parahaemolyticus under pure culture conditions and 6.1 × 10 cfu/mL in artificially contaminated cod samples, providing fast response times, selectivity and high sensitivity for the on-site quantification of V. parahaemolyticus residues in real food (e.g. fish) matrices. In addition, CDs as fluorescent donors are synthesized in simple steps and have properties such as high fluorescence intensity, superior biocompatible properties and resistance to photobleaching. The fluorescence quenching agent AuNPs has properties such as large surface area to volume ratio, easy surface functionalization and efficient fluorescence quenching. At the same time, the paper-based use of cellulose materials was far less costly than traditional materials such as plexiglas, silicone, and has good biocompatibility. Therefore, the paper sensor proposed in this study has promising applications in the field detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest related to the work reported in this manuscript. All authors have read and agreed to submit the manuscript.

Yuwei Ren: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Lulu Cao: Data curation, Validation. Xiyan Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Rui Jiao: Software. Dexin Ou: Discussion. Yang Wang: Investigation. Danfeng Zhang: Validation, Investigation. Yizhong Shen: Formal analysis. Na Ling: Methodology. Yingwang Ye: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (32102104); and the Outstanding Youth Fund of Anhui Natural Science Foundation (2208085J11).
